NKJV.1: He also said to His disciples: “There was a certain rich man who had a steward, and an accusation was brought to him that this man was wasting his goods.
NKJV.2: So he called him and said to him, ‘What is this I hear about you? Give an  account of your stewardship, for you can no longer be steward.’
NKJV.3: “Then the steward said within himself, ‘What shall I do? For my master is taking the stewardship away from me. I cannot dig; I am ashamed to beg.
NKJV.4: I have resolved what to do, that when I am put out of the stewardship, they may receive me into their houses.’
NKJV.5: “So he called every one of his master’s debtors to him, and said to the first, ‘How much do you owe my master?’
NKJV.6: And he said, ‘A hundred measures of oil.’ So he said to him, ‘Take your bill, and sit down quickly and write fifty.’
NKJV.7: Then he said to another, ‘And how much do you owe?’ So he said, ‘A hundred measures of wheat.’ And he said to him, ‘Take your bill, and write eighty.’
NKJV.8: So the master commended the unjust steward because he had dealt shrewdly. For the sons of this world are more shrewd in their generation than  the sons of light.
NKJV.9: “And I say to you,  make friends for yourselves by unrighteous mammon, that when you fail, they may receive you into an everlasting home.
NKJV.10: He who is faithful in what is least is faithful also in much; and he who is unjust in what is least is unjust also in much.
NKJV.11: Therefore if you have not been faithful in the unrighteous mammon, who will commit to your trust the true riches?
NKJV.12: And if you have not been faithful in what is another man’s, who will give you what is your  own?
NKJV.13: “No servant can serve two masters; for either he will hate the one and love the other, or else he will be loyal to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and mammon.”
NKJV.14: Now the Pharisees, who were lovers of money, also heard all these things, and they derided Him.
NKJV.15: And He said to them, “You are those who  justify yourselves  before men, but  God knows your hearts. For  what is highly esteemed among men is an abomination in the sight of God.
NKJV.16: “The law and the prophets were until John. Since that time the kingdom of God has been preached, and everyone is pressing into it.
NKJV.17: And it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away than for one tittle of the law to fail.
NKJV.18: “Whoever divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery; and whoever marries her who is divorced from her husband commits adultery.
NKJV.19: “There was a certain rich man who was clothed in purple and fine linen and fared sumptuously every day.
NKJV.20: But there was a certain beggar named Lazarus, full of sores, who was laid at his gate,
NKJV.21: desiring to be fed with the crumbs which fell from the rich man’s table. Moreover the dogs came and licked his sores.
NKJV.22: So it was that the beggar died, and was carried by the angels to  Abraham’s bosom. The rich man also died and was buried.
NKJV.23: And being in torments in Hades, he lifted up his eyes and saw Abraham afar off, and Lazarus in his bosom.
NKJV.24: “Then he cried and said, ‘Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus that he may dip the tip of his finger in water and  cool my tongue; for I  am tormented in this flame.’
NKJV.25: But Abraham said, ‘Son,  remember that in your lifetime you received your good things, and likewise Lazarus evil things; but now he is comforted and you are tormented.
NKJV.26: And besides all this, between us and you there is a great gulf fixed, so that those who want to pass from here to you cannot, nor can those from there pass to us.’
NKJV.27: “Then he said, ‘I beg you therefore, father, that you would send him to my father’s house,
NKJV.28: for I have five brothers, that he may testify to them, lest they also come to this place of torment.’
NKJV.29: Abraham said to him,  ‘They have Moses and the prophets; let them hear them.’
NKJV.30: And he said, ‘No, father Abraham; but if one goes to them from the dead, they will repent.’
NKJV.31: But he said to him,  ‘If they do not hear Moses and the prophets,  neither will they be persuaded though one rise from the dead.’ ”

ESV.1: He also said to the disciples, “There was a rich man who had a manager, and charges were brought to him that this man was wasting his possessions.
ESV.2: And he called him and said to him, ‘What is this that I hear about you? Turn in the account of your management, for you can no longer be manager.’
ESV.3: And the manager said to himself, ‘What shall I do, since my master is taking the management away from me? I am not strong enough to dig, and I am ashamed to beg.
ESV.4: I have decided what to do, so that when I am removed from management, people may receive me into their houses.’
ESV.5: So, summoning his master's debtors one by one, he said to the first, ‘How much do you owe my master?’
ESV.6: He said, ‘A hundred measures  of oil.’ He said to him, ‘Take your bill, and sit down quickly and write fifty.’
ESV.7: Then he said to another, ‘And how much do you owe?’ He said, ‘A hundred measures  of wheat.’ He said to him, ‘Take your bill, and write eighty.’
ESV.8: The master commended the dishonest manager for his shrewdness. For the sons of this world  are more shrewd in dealing with their own generation than the sons of light.
ESV.9: And I tell you, make friends for yourselves by means of unrighteous wealth,  so that when it fails they may receive you into the eternal dwellings.
ESV.10: “One who is faithful in a very little is also faithful in much, and one who is dishonest in a very little is also dishonest in much.
ESV.11: If then you have not been faithful in the unrighteous wealth, who will entrust to you the true riches?
ESV.12: And if you have not been faithful in that which is another's, who will give you that which is your own?
ESV.13: No servant can serve two masters, for either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and money.”
ESV.14: The Pharisees, who were lovers of money, heard all these things, and they ridiculed him.
ESV.15: And he said to them, “You are those who justify yourselves before men, but God knows your hearts. For what is exalted among men is an abomination in the sight of God.
ESV.16: “The Law and the Prophets were until John; since then the good news of the kingdom of God is preached, and everyone forces his way into it.
ESV.17: But it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away than for one dot of the Law to become void.
ESV.18: “Everyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery, and he who marries a woman divorced from her husband commits adultery.
ESV.19: “There was a rich man who was clothed in purple and fine linen and who feasted sumptuously every day.
ESV.20: And at his gate was laid a poor man named Lazarus, covered with sores,
ESV.21: who desired to be fed with what fell from the rich man's table. Moreover, even the dogs came and licked his sores.
ESV.22: The poor man died and was carried by the angels to Abraham's side.  The rich man also died and was buried,
ESV.23: and in Hades, being in torment, he lifted up his eyes and saw Abraham far off and Lazarus at his side.
ESV.24: And he called out, ‘Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus to dip the end of his finger in water and cool my tongue, for I am in anguish in this flame.’
ESV.25: But Abraham said, ‘Child, remember that you in your lifetime received your good things, and Lazarus in like manner bad things; but now he is comforted here, and you are in anguish.
ESV.26: And besides all this, between us and you a great chasm has been fixed, in order that those who would pass from here to you may not be able, and none may cross from there to us.’
ESV.27: And he said, ‘Then I beg you, father, to send him to my father's house —
ESV.28: for I have five brothers — so that he may warn them, lest they also come into this place of torment.’
ESV.29: But Abraham said, ‘They have Moses and the Prophets; let them hear them.’
ESV.30: And he said, ‘No, father Abraham, but if someone goes to them from the dead, they will repent.’
ESV.31: He said to him, ‘If they do not hear Moses and the Prophets, neither will they be convinced if someone should rise from the dead.’”

NASB.1: Now He was also saying to the disciples, 'There was a rich man who had a manager, and this [manager] was reported to him as squandering his possessions.
NASB.2: 'And he called him and said to him, 'What is this I hear about you? Give an accounting of your management, for you can no longer be manager.'
NASB.3: 'The manager said to himself, 'What shall I do, since my master is taking the management away from me? I am not strong enough to dig; I am ashamed to beg.
NASB.4: 'I know what I shall do, so that when I am removed from the management people will welcome me into their homes.'
NASB.5: 'And he summoned each one of his master's debtors, and he [began] saying to the first, 'How much do you owe my master?'
NASB.6: 'And he said, 'A hundred measures of oil.' And he said to him, 'Take your bill, and sit down quickly and write fifty.'
NASB.7: 'Then he said to another, 'And how much do you owe?' And he said, 'A hundred measures of wheat.' He said to him, 'Take your bill, and write eighty.'
NASB.8: 'And his master praised the unrighteous manager because he had acted shrewdly; for the sons of this age are more shrewd in relation to their own kind than the sons of light.
NASB.9: 'And I say to you, make friends for yourselves by means of the wealth of unrighteousness, so that when it fails, they will receive you into the eternal dwellings.
NASB.10: 'He who is faithful in a very little thing is faithful also in much; and he who is unrighteous in a very little thing is unrighteous also in much.
NASB.11: 'Therefore if you have not been faithful in the [use of] unrighteous wealth, who will entrust the true [riches] to you?
NASB.12: 'And if you have not been faithful in [the use of] that which is another's, who will give you that which is your own?
NASB.13: 'No servant can serve two masters; for either he will hate the one and love the other, or else he will be devoted to one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and wealth.'
NASB.14: Now the Pharisees, who were lovers of money, were listening to all these things and were scoffing at Him.
NASB.15: And He said to them, 'You are those who justify yourselves in the sight of men, but God knows your hearts; for that which is highly esteemed among men is detestable in the sight of God.
NASB.16: 'The Law and the Prophets [were proclaimed] until John; since that time the gospel of the kingdom of God has been preached, and everyone is forcing his way into it.
NASB.17: 'But it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away than for one stroke of a letter of the Law to fail.
NASB.18: 'Everyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery, and he who marries one who is divorced from a husband commits adultery.
NASB.19: 'Now there was a rich man, and he habitually dressed in purple and fine linen, joyously living in splendor every day.
NASB.20: 'And a poor man named Lazarus was laid at his gate, covered with sores,
NASB.21: and longing to be fed with the [crumbs] which were falling from the rich man's table; besides, even the dogs were coming and licking his sores.
NASB.22: 'Now the poor man died and was carried away by the angels to Abraham's bosom; and the rich man also died and was buried.
NASB.23: 'In Hades he lifted up his eyes, being in torment, and saw Abraham far away and Lazarus in his bosom.
NASB.24: 'And he cried out and said, 'Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus so that he may dip the tip of his finger in water and cool off my tongue, for I am in agony in this flame.'
NASB.25: 'But Abraham said, 'Child, remember that during your life you received your good things, and likewise Lazarus bad things; but now he is being comforted here, and you are in agony.
NASB.26: 'And besides all this, between us and you there is a great chasm fixed, so that those who wish to come over from here to you will not be able, and [that] none may cross over from there to us.'
NASB.27: 'And he said, 'Then I beg you, father, that you send him to my father's house--
NASB.28: for I have five brothers-- in order that he may warn them, so that they will not also come to this place of torment.'
NASB.29: 'But Abraham said, 'They have Moses and the Prophets; let them hear them.'
NASB.30: 'But he said, 'No, father Abraham, but if someone goes to them from the dead, they will repent!'
NASB.31: 'But he said to him, 'If they do not listen to Moses and the Prophets, they will not be persuaded even if someone rises from the dead.''

NLT.1: Jesus told this story to his disciples: “There was a certain rich man who had a manager handling his affairs. One day a report came that the manager was wasting his employer’s money.
NLT.2: So the employer called him in and said, ‘What’s this I hear about you? Get your report in order, because you are going to be fired.’
NLT.3: “The manager thought to himself, ‘Now what? My boss has fired me. I don’t have the strength to dig ditches, and I’m too proud to beg.
NLT.4: Ah, I know how to ensure that I’ll have plenty of friends who will give me a home when I am fired.’
NLT.5: “So he invited each person who owed money to his employer to come and discuss the situation. He asked the first one, ‘How much do you owe him?’
NLT.6: The man replied, ‘I owe him 800 gallons of olive oil.’ So the manager told him, ‘Take the bill and quickly change it to 400 gallons. ’
NLT.7: “‘And how much do you owe my employer?’ he asked the next man. ‘I owe him 1,000 bushels of wheat,’ was the reply. ‘Here,’ the manager said, ‘take the bill and change it to 800 bushels. ’
NLT.8: “The rich man had to admire the dishonest rascal for being so shrewd. And it is true that the children of this world are more shrewd in dealing with the world around them than are the children of the light.
NLT.9: Here’s the lesson: Use your worldly resources to benefit others and make friends. Then, when your possessions are gone, they will welcome you to an eternal home.
NLT.10: “If you are faithful in little things, you will be faithful in large ones. But if you are dishonest in little things, you won’t be honest with greater responsibilities.
NLT.11: And if you are untrustworthy about worldly wealth, who will trust you with the true riches of heaven?
NLT.12: And if you are not faithful with other people’s things, why should you be trusted with things of your own?
NLT.13: “No one can serve two masters. For you will hate one and love the other; you will be devoted to one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and be enslaved to money.”
NLT.14: The Pharisees, who dearly loved their money, heard all this and scoffed at him.
NLT.15: Then he said to them, “You like to appear righteous in public, but God knows your hearts. What this world honors is detestable in the sight of God.
NLT.16: “Until John the Baptist, the law of Moses and the messages of the prophets were your guides. But now the Good News of the Kingdom of God is preached, and everyone is eager to get in.
NLT.17: But that doesn’t mean that the law has lost its force. It is easier for heaven and earth to disappear than for the smallest point of God’s law to be overturned.
NLT.18: “For example, a man who divorces his wife and marries someone else commits adultery. And anyone who marries a woman divorced from her husband commits adultery.”
NLT.19: Jesus said, “There was a certain rich man who was splendidly clothed in purple and fine linen and who lived each day in luxury.
NLT.20: At his gate lay a poor man named Lazarus who was covered with sores.
NLT.21: As Lazarus lay there longing for scraps from the rich man’s table, the dogs would come and lick his open sores.
NLT.22: “Finally, the poor man died and was carried by the angels to sit beside Abraham at the heavenly banquet. The rich man also died and was buried,
NLT.23: and he went to the place of the dead. There, in torment, he saw Abraham in the far distance with Lazarus at his side.
NLT.24: “The rich man shouted, ‘Father Abraham, have some pity! Send Lazarus over here to dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue. I am in anguish in these flames.’
NLT.25: “But Abraham said to him, ‘Son, remember that during your lifetime you had everything you wanted, and Lazarus had nothing. So now he is here being comforted, and you are in anguish.
NLT.26: And besides, there is a great chasm separating us. No one can cross over to you from here, and no one can cross over to us from there.’
NLT.27: “Then the rich man said, ‘Please, Father Abraham, at least send him to my father’s home.
NLT.28: For I have five brothers, and I want him to warn them so they don’t end up in this place of torment.’
NLT.29: “But Abraham said, ‘Moses and the prophets have warned them. Your brothers can read what they wrote.’
NLT.30: “The rich man replied, ‘No, Father Abraham! But if someone is sent to them from the dead, then they will repent of their sins and turn to God.’
NLT.31: “But Abraham said, ‘If they won’t listen to Moses and the prophets, they won’t be persuaded even if someone rises from the dead.’”

WEB.1: He also said to his disciples, “There was a certain rich man who had a manager. An accusation was made to him that this man was wasting his possessions.
WEB.2: He called him, and said to him, ‘What is this that I hear about you? Give an accounting of your management, for you can no longer be manager.’
WEB.3: “The manager said within himself, ‘What will I do, seeing that my lord is taking away the management position from me? I don’t have strength to dig. I am ashamed to beg.
WEB.4: I know what I will do, so that when I am removed from management, they may receive me into their houses.’
WEB.5: Calling each one of his lord’s debtors to him, he said to the first, ‘How much do you owe to my lord?’
WEB.6: He said, ‘A hundred batos  of oil.’ He said to him, ‘Take your bill, and sit down quickly and write fifty.’
WEB.7: Then he said to another, ‘How much do you owe?’ He said, ‘A hundred cors  of wheat.’ He said to him, ‘Take your bill, and write eighty.’
WEB.8: “His lord commended the dishonest manager because he had done wisely, for the children of this world are, in their own generation, wiser than the children of the light.
WEB.9: I tell you, make for yourselves friends by means of unrighteous mammon, so that when you fail, they may receive you into the eternal tents.
WEB.10: He who is faithful in a very little is faithful also in much. He who is dishonest in a very little is also dishonest in much.
WEB.11: If therefore you have not been faithful in the unrighteous mammon, who will commit to your trust the true riches?
WEB.12: If you have not been faithful in that which is another’s, who will give you that which is your own?
WEB.13: No servant can serve two masters, for either he will hate the one, and love the other; or else he will hold to one, and despise the other. You aren’t able to serve God and Mammon.”
WEB.14: The Pharisees, who were lovers of money, also heard all these things, and they scoffed at him.
WEB.15: He said to them, “You are those who justify yourselves in the sight of men, but God knows your hearts. For that which is exalted among men is an abomination in the sight of God.
WEB.16: The law and the prophets were until John. From that time the Good News of God’s Kingdom is preached, and everyone is forcing his way into it.
WEB.17: But it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away, than for one tiny stroke of a pen in the law to fall.
WEB.18: Everyone who divorces his wife, and marries another, commits adultery. He who marries one who is divorced from a husband commits adultery.
WEB.19: “Now there was a certain rich man, and he was clothed in purple and fine linen, living in luxury every day.
WEB.20: A certain beggar, named Lazarus, was laid at his gate, full of sores,
WEB.21: and desiring to be fed with the crumbs that fell from the rich man’s table. Yes, even the dogs came and licked his sores.
WEB.22: The beggar died, and he was carried away by the angels to Abraham’s bosom. The rich man also died, and was buried.
WEB.23: In Hades,  he lifted up his eyes, being in torment, and saw Abraham far off, and Lazarus at his bosom.
WEB.24: He cried and said, ‘Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus, that he may dip the tip of his finger in water, and cool my tongue! For I am in anguish in this flame.’
WEB.25: “But Abraham said, ‘Son, remember that you, in your lifetime, received your good things, and Lazarus, in the same way, bad things. But now here he is comforted and you are in anguish.
WEB.26: Besides all this, between us and you there is a great gulf fixed, that those who want to pass from here to you are not able, and that no one may cross over from there to us.’
WEB.27: “He said, ‘I ask you therefore, father, that you would send him to my father’s house;
WEB.28: for I have five brothers, that he may testify to them, so they won’t also come into this place of torment.’
WEB.29: “But Abraham said to him, ‘They have Moses and the prophets. Let them listen to them.’
WEB.30: “He said, ‘No, father Abraham, but if one goes to them from the dead, they will repent.’
WEB.31: “He said to him, ‘If they don’t listen to Moses and the prophets, neither will they be persuaded if one rises from the dead.’”

CSB17.1: Now he said to the disciples: “There was a rich <sup>ⓕ</sup>man who received an accusation that his manager <sup>ⓖ</sup>was squandering <sup>ⓗ</sup>his possessions.
CSB17.2: So he called the manager in and asked, ‘What is this I hear about you? Give an account of your management, <sup>ⓘ</sup>because you can no longer be my manager.’
CSB17.3: “Then the manager said to himself, ‘What will I do since my master is taking the management away from me? I’m not strong enough to dig; I’m ashamed to beg.
CSB17.4: I know what I’ll do so that when I’m removed from management, people will welcome me into their homes.’
CSB17.5: “So he summoned each one of his master’s debtors. ‘How much do you owe my master? ’ he asked the first one.
CSB17.6: “‘A hundred measures of olive oil,’ he said.  “‘Take your invoice,’ he told him, ‘sit down quickly, and write fifty.’
CSB17.7: “Next he asked another, ‘How much do you owe?’  “‘A hundred measures of wheat,’ he said.  “‘Take your invoice,’ he told him, ‘and write eighty.’
CSB17.8: “The master praised the unrighteous manager <sup>ⓙ</sup> because he had acted shrewdly. For the children of this age <sup>ⓚ</sup> are more shrewd than the children of light <sup>ⓛ</sup> in dealing with their own people. <sup>[1]</sup>
CSB17.9: And I tell you, make friends <sup>ⓜ</sup>for yourselves by means of worldly wealth <sup>[2]</sup> <sup>ⓝ</sup>so that when it fails, <sup>[3]</sup>they may welcome you into eternal dwellings.
CSB17.10: Whoever is faithful <sup>ⓐ</sup> in very little <sup>ⓑ</sup>is also faithful in much, and whoever is unrighteous in very little is also unrighteous in much.
CSB17.11: So if you have not been faithful with worldly wealth, who will trust you with what is genuine?
CSB17.12: And if you have not been faithful with what belongs to someone else, who will give you what is your own?
CSB17.13: No <sup>ⓒ</sup>servant can serve two masters, since either he will hate <sup>ⓓ</sup>one and love the other, or he will be devoted to one and despise the other. You cannot serve both God and money.”
CSB17.14: The Pharisees, who were lovers of money, <sup>ⓔ</sup> were listening to all these things and scoffing <sup>ⓕ</sup> at him.
CSB17.15: And he told them, “You are the ones who justify <sup>ⓖ</sup>yourselves in the sight of others, but God knows your hearts. <sup>ⓗ</sup>For what is highly admired by people is revolting <sup>ⓘ</sup>in God’s sight.
CSB17.16: “The <sup>ⓙ</sup> Law and the Prophets <sup>ⓚ</sup> were until John; since then, the good news of the kingdom of God <sup>ⓛ</sup> has been proclaimed, and everyone is urgently invited to enter it. <sup>[4]</sup>
CSB17.17: But it is easier <sup>ⓜ</sup>for heaven and earth to pass away than for one stroke of a letter in the law to drop out.
CSB17.18: “Everyone <sup>ⓝ</sup> who divorces <sup>ⓐ</sup> his wife and marries another woman commits adultery, <sup>ⓑ</sup> and everyone who marries a woman divorced from her husband commits adultery.
CSB17.19: “There was a rich man who would dress in purple and fine linen, <sup>ⓒ</sup> feasting lavishly every day.
CSB17.20: But a poor man named Lazarus, covered with sores, was lying at his gate. <sup>ⓓ</sup>
CSB17.21: He longed to be filled <sup>ⓔ</sup>with what fell from the rich man’s table, <sup>ⓕ</sup>but instead the dogs <sup>ⓖ</sup>would come and lick his sores.
CSB17.22: One day the poor man died and was carried away by the angels to Abraham’s side. <sup>[5]</sup> <sup>ⓗ</sup>The rich man also died and was buried.
CSB17.23: And being in torment <sup>ⓘ</sup> in Hades, <sup>ⓙ</sup>he looked up and saw Abraham a long way off, with Lazarus at his side.
CSB17.24: ‘Father Abraham! ’  <sup>ⓚ</sup>he called out, ‘Have mercy on me and send Lazarus to dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue, because I am in agony <sup>ⓛ</sup>in this flame!’
CSB17.25: “‘Son,’ <sup>[6]</sup> Abraham said, ‘remember that during your life you received your good things, <sup>ⓜ</sup> just as Lazarus received bad things, but now he is comforted here, <sup>ⓝ</sup> while you are in agony.
CSB17.26: Besides all this, a great chasm has been fixed between us and you, so that those who want to pass over from here to you cannot; neither can those from there cross over to us.’
CSB17.27: “‘Father,’ he said, ‘then I beg you to send him to my father’s house —
CSB17.28: because I have five brothers — to warn <sup>ⓐ</sup>them, so they won’t also come to this place of torment.’
CSB17.29: “But Abraham said, ‘They have Moses and the prophets;  <sup>ⓑ</sup> they should listen to them.’
CSB17.30: “‘No, father Abraham,’ he said. ‘But if someone from the dead goes to them, they will repent.’
CSB17.31: “But he told him, ‘If they don’t listen to Moses and the prophets, they will not be persuaded if someone rises from the dead.’” <sup>ⓒ</sup>

AMP.1: Now Jesus was also saying to the disciples, "There was a certain rich man who had a manager [of his estate], and accusations [against this man] were brought to him, that this man was squandering his [master's] possessions.
AMP.2: So he called him and said to him, 'What is this I hear about you? Give an accounting of your management [of my affairs], for you can no longer be [my] manager.'
AMP.3: The manager [of the estate] said to himself, 'What will I do, since my master is taking the management away from me? I am not strong enough to dig [for a living], and I am ashamed to beg.
AMP.4: I know what I will do, so that when I am removed from the management, people [who are my master's debtors] will welcome me into their homes.'
AMP.5: So he summoned his master's debtors one by one, and he said to the first, 'How much do you owe my master?'
AMP.6: He said, 'A hundred measures of [olive] oil.' And he said to him, 'Take your bill, and sit down quickly and write fifty.'
AMP.7: Then he said to another, 'And how much do you owe?' He said, 'A hundred measures of wheat.' He said to him, 'Take your bill, and write eighty.'
AMP.8: And his master commended the unjust manager [not for his misdeeds, but] because he had acted shrewdly [by preparing for his future unemployment]; for the sons of this age [the non-believers] are shrewder in relation to their own kind [that is, to the ways of the secular world] than are the sons of light [the believers].
AMP.9: And I tell you [learn from this], make friends for yourselves [for eternity] by means of the wealth of unrighteousness [that is, use material resources as a way to further the work of God], so that when it runs out, they will welcome you into the eternal dwellings.
AMP.10: "He who is faithful in a very little thing is also faithful in much; and he who is dishonest in a very little thing is also dishonest in much.
AMP.11: Therefore if you have not been faithful in the use of earthly wealth, who will entrust the true riches to you?
AMP.12: And if you have not been faithful in the use of that [earthly wealth] which belongs to another [whether God or man, and of which you are a trustee], who will give you that which is your own?
AMP.13: No servant can serve two masters; for either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will stand devotedly by the one and despise the other. You cannot serve both God and mammon [that is, your earthly possessions or anything else you trust in and rely on instead of God]."
AMP.14: Now the Pharisees, who were lovers of money, were listening to all these things and were sneering and ridiculing Him.
AMP.15: So He said to them, "You are the ones who declare yourselves just and upright in the sight of men, but God knows your hearts [your thoughts, your desires, your secrets]; for that which is highly esteemed among men is detestable in the sight of God.
AMP.16: "The Law and the [writings of the] Prophets were proclaimed until John; since then the gospel of the kingdom of God has been and continues to be preached, and everyone tries forcefully to go into it.
AMP.17: Yet it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away than for a single stroke of a letter of the Law to fail and become void.
AMP.18: "Whoever divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery, and he who marries one who is divorced from her husband commits adultery.
AMP.19: "Now there was a certain rich man who was habitually dressed in expensive purple and fine linen, and celebrated and lived joyously in splendor every day.
AMP.20: And a poor man named Lazarus, was laid at his gate, covered with sores.
AMP.21: He [eagerly] longed to eat the crumbs which fell from the rich man's table. Besides, even the dogs were coming and licking his sores.
AMP.22: Now it happened that the poor man died and his spirit was carried away by the angels to Abraham's bosom (paradise); and the rich man also died and was buried.
AMP.23: In Hades (the realm of the dead), being in torment, he looked up and saw Abraham far away and Lazarus in his bosom (paradise).
AMP.24: And he cried out, 'Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus so that he may dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue, because I am in severe agony in this flame.'
AMP.25: But Abraham said, 'Son, remember that in your lifetime you received your good things [all the comforts and delights], and Lazarus likewise bad things [all the discomforts and distresses]; but now he is comforted here [in paradise], while you are in severe agony.
AMP.26: And besides all this, between us and you [people] a great chasm has been fixed, so that those who want to come over from here to you will not be able, and none may cross over from there to us.'
AMP.27: So the rich man said, 'Then, father [Abraham], I beg you to send Lazarus to my father's house—
AMP.28: for I have five brothers—in order that he may solemnly warn them and witness to them, so that they too will not come to this place of torment.'
AMP.29: But Abraham said, 'They have [the Scriptures given by] Moses and the [writings of the] Prophets; let them listen to them.'
AMP.30: He replied, 'No, father Abraham, but if someone from the dead goes to them, they will repent [they will change their old way of thinking and seek God and His righteousness].'
AMP.31: And he said to him, 'If they do not listen to [the messages of] Moses and the Prophets, they will not be persuaded even if someone rises from the dead.'"

MSG.1: Jesus said to his disciples, "There was once a rich man who had a manager. He got reports that the manager had been taking advantage of his position by running up huge personal expenses.
MSG.2: So he called him in and said, 'What's this I hear about you? You're fired. And I want a complete audit of your books.'
MSG.3: "The manager said to himself, 'What am I going to do? I've lost my job as manager. I'm not strong enough for a laboring job, and I'm too proud to beg... .
MSG.4: Ah, I've got a plan. Here's what I'll do... then when I'm turned out into the street, people will take me into their houses.'
MSG.5: "Then he went at it. One after another, he called in the people who were in debt to his master. He said to the first, 'How much do you owe my master?'
MSG.6: "He replied, 'A hundred jugs of olive oil.' "The manager said, 'Here, take your bill, sit down here—quick now—write fifty.'
MSG.7: "To the next he said, 'And you, what do you owe?' "He answered, 'A hundred sacks of wheat.' "He said, 'Take your bill, write in eighty.'
MSG.8: "Now here's a surprise: The master praised the crooked manager! And why? Because he knew how to look after himself. Streetwise people are smarter in this regard than law-abiding citizens. They are on constant alert, looking for angles, surviving by their wits.
MSG.9: I want you to be smart in the same way—but for what is right—using every adversity to stimulate you to creative survival, to concentrate your attention on the bare essentials, so you'll live, really live, and not complacently just get by on good behavior."
MSG.10: Jesus went on to make these comments: If you're honest in small things, you'll be honest in big things;
MSG.11: If you're a crook in small things, you'll be a crook in big things.
MSG.12: If you're not honest in small jobs, who will put you in charge of the store?
MSG.13: No worker can serve two bosses: He'll either hate the first and love the second Or adore the first and despise the second. You can't serve both God and the Bank.
MSG.14: When the Pharisees, a money-obsessed bunch, heard him say these things, they rolled their eyes, dismissing him as hopelessly out of touch.
MSG.15: So Jesus spoke to them: "You are masters at making yourselves look good in front of others, but God knows what's behind the appearance. What society sees and calls monumental, God sees through and calls monstrous.
MSG.16: God's Law and the Prophets climaxed in John; Now it's all kingdom of God—the glad news and compelling invitation to every man and woman.
MSG.17: The sky will disintegrate and the earth dissolve before a single letter of God's Law wears out.
MSG.18: Using the legalities of divorce as a cover for lust is adultery; Using the legalities of marriage as a cover for lust is adultery.
MSG.19: "There once was a rich man, expensively dressed in the latest fashions, wasting his days in conspicuous consumption.
MSG.20: A poor man named Lazarus, covered with sores, had been dumped on his doorstep.
MSG.21: All he lived for was to get a meal from scraps off the rich man's table. His best friends were the dogs who came and licked his sores.
MSG.22: "Then he died, this poor man, and was taken up by the angels to the lap of Abraham. The rich man also died and was buried.
MSG.23: In hell and in torment, he looked up and saw Abraham in the distance and Lazarus in his lap.
MSG.24: He called out, 'Father Abraham, mercy! Have mercy! Send Lazarus to dip his finger in water to cool my tongue. I'm in agony in this fire.'
MSG.25: "But Abraham said, 'Child, remember that in your lifetime you got the good things and Lazarus the bad things. It's not like that here. Here he's consoled and you're tormented.
MSG.26: Besides, in all these matters there is a huge chasm set between us so that no one can go from us to you even if he wanted to, nor can anyone cross over from you to us.'
MSG.27: "The rich man said, 'Then let me ask you, Father: Send him to the house of my father
MSG.28: where I have five brothers, so he can tell them the score and warn them so they won't end up here in this place of torment.'
MSG.29: "Abraham answered, 'They have Moses and the Prophets to tell them the score. Let them listen to them.'
MSG.30: " 'I know, Father Abraham,' he said, 'but they're not listening. If someone came back to them from the dead, they would change their ways.'
MSG.31: "Abraham replied, 'If they won't listen to Moses and the Prophets, they're not going to be convinced by someone who rises from the dead.'"

NIV.1:
The Parable of the Shrewd Manager
Jesus told his disciples: “There was a rich man whose manager was accused of wasting his possessions.
NIV.2: So he called him in and asked him, ‘What is this I hear about you? Give an account of your management, because you cannot be manager any longer.’
NIV.3: “The manager said to himself, ‘What shall I do now? My master is taking away my job. I’m not strong enough to dig, and I’m ashamed to beg—
NIV.4: I know what I’ll do so that, when I lose my job here, people will welcome me into their houses.’
NIV.5: “So he called in each one of his master’s debtors. He asked the first, ‘How much do you owe my master?’
NIV.6:
“‘Eight hundred gallons of olive oil,’ he replied.
“The manager told him, ‘Take your bill, sit down quickly, and make it four hundred.’
NIV.7:
“Then he asked the second, ‘And how much do you owe?’
“‘A thousand bushels of wheat,’ he replied.
“He told him, ‘Take your bill and make it eight hundred.’
NIV.8: “The master commended the dishonest manager because he had acted shrewdly. For the people of this world are more shrewd in dealing with their own kind than are the people of the light.
NIV.9: I tell you, use worldly wealth to gain friends for yourselves, so that when it is gone, you will be welcomed into eternal dwellings.
NIV.10: “Whoever can be trusted with very little can also be trusted with much, and whoever is dishonest with very little will also be dishonest with much.
NIV.11: So if you have not been trustworthy in handling worldly wealth, who will trust you with true riches?
NIV.12: And if you have not been trustworthy with someone else’s property, who will give you property of your own?
NIV.13: “No servant can serve two masters. Either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve both God and Money.”
NIV.14: The Pharisees, who loved money, heard all this and were sneering at Jesus.
NIV.15: He said to them, “You are the ones who justify yourselves in the eyes of men, but God knows your hearts. What is highly valued among men is detestable in God’s sight.
NIV.16:
Additional Teachings
“The Law and the Prophets were proclaimed until John. Since that time, the good news of the kingdom of God is being preached, and everyone is forcing his way into it.
NIV.17: It is easier for heaven and earth to disappear than for the least stroke of a pen to drop out of the Law.
NIV.18: “Anyone who divorces his wife and marries another woman commits adultery, and the man who marries a divorced woman commits adultery.
NIV.19:
The Rich Man and Lazarus
“There was a rich man who was dressed in purple and fine linen and lived in luxury every day.
NIV.20: At his gate was laid a beggar named Lazarus, covered with sores
NIV.21: and longing to eat what fell from the rich man’s table. Even the dogs came and licked his sores.
NIV.22: “The time came when the beggar died and the angels carried him to Abraham’s side. The rich man also died and was buried.
NIV.23: In hell, where he was in torment, he looked up and saw Abraham far away, with Lazarus by his side.
NIV.24: So he called to him, ‘Father Abraham, have pity on me and send Lazarus to dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue, because I am in agony in this fire.’
NIV.25: “But Abraham replied, ‘Son, remember that in your lifetime you received your good things, while Lazarus received bad things, but now he is comforted here and you are in agony.
NIV.26: And besides all this, between us and you a great chasm has been fixed, so that those who want to go from here to you cannot, nor can anyone cross over from there to us.’
NIV.27: “He answered, ‘Then I beg you, father, send Lazarus to my father’s house,
NIV.28: for I have five brothers. Let him warn them, so that they will not also come to this place of torment.’
NIV.29: “Abraham replied, ‘They have Moses and the Prophets; let them listen to them.’
NIV.30: “‘No, father Abraham,’ he said, ‘but if someone from the dead goes to them, they will repent.’
NIV.31: “He said to him, ‘If they do not listen to Moses and the Prophets, they will not be convinced even if someone rises from the dead.’”

NRSVCE.1: Then Jesus said to the disciples, “There was a rich man who had a manager, and charges were brought to him that this man was squandering his property.
NRSVCE.2: So he summoned him and said to him, ‘What is this that I hear about you? Give me an accounting of your management, because you cannot be my manager any longer.’
NRSVCE.3: Then the manager said to himself, ‘What will I do, now that my master is taking the position away from me? I am not strong enough to dig, and I am ashamed to beg.
NRSVCE.4: I have decided what to do so that, when I am dismissed as manager, people may welcome me into their homes.’
NRSVCE.5: So, summoning his master’s debtors one by one, he asked the first, ‘How much do you owe my master?’
NRSVCE.6: He answered, ‘A hundred jugs of olive oil.’ He said to him, ‘Take your bill, sit down quickly, and make it fifty.’
NRSVCE.7: Then he asked another, ‘And how much do you owe?’ He replied, ‘A hundred containers of wheat.’ He said to him, ‘Take your bill and make it eighty.’
NRSVCE.8: And his master commended the dishonest manager because he had acted shrewdly; for the children of this age are more shrewd in dealing with their own generation than are the children of light.
NRSVCE.9: And I tell you, make friends for yourselves by means of dishonest wealth so that when it is gone, they may welcome you into the eternal homes.
NRSVCE.10: “Whoever is faithful in a very little is faithful also in much; and whoever is dishonest in a very little is dishonest also in much.
NRSVCE.11: If then you have not been faithful with the dishonest wealth, who will entrust to you the true riches?
NRSVCE.12: And if you have not been faithful with what belongs to another, who will give you what is your own?
NRSVCE.13: No slave can serve two masters; for a slave will either hate the one and love the other, or be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and wealth.”
NRSVCE.14: The Pharisees, who were lovers of money, heard all this, and they ridiculed him.
NRSVCE.15: So he said to them, “You are those who justify yourselves in the sight of others; but God knows your hearts; for what is prized by human beings is an abomination in the sight of God.
NRSVCE.16: “The law and the prophets were in effect until John came; since then the good news of the kingdom of God is proclaimed, and everyone tries to enter it by force.
NRSVCE.17: But it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away, than for one stroke of a letter in the law to be dropped.
NRSVCE.18: “Anyone who divorces his wife and marries another commits adultery, and whoever marries a woman divorced from her husband commits adultery.
NRSVCE.19: “There was a rich man who was dressed in purple and fine linen and who feasted sumptuously every day.
NRSVCE.20: And at his gate lay a poor man named Lazarus, covered with sores,
NRSVCE.21: who longed to satisfy his hunger with what fell from the rich man’s table; even the dogs would come and lick his sores.
NRSVCE.22: The poor man died and was carried away by the angels to be with Abraham. The rich man also died and was buried.
NRSVCE.23: In Hades, where he was being tormented, he looked up and saw Abraham far away with Lazarus by his side.
NRSVCE.24: He called out, ‘Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus to dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue; for I am in agony in these flames.’
NRSVCE.25: But Abraham said, ‘Child, remember that during your lifetime you received your good things, and Lazarus in like manner evil things; but now he is comforted here, and you are in agony.
NRSVCE.26: Besides all this, between you and us a great chasm has been fixed, so that those who might want to pass from here to you cannot do so, and no one can cross from there to us.’
NRSVCE.27: He said, ‘Then, father, I beg you to send him to my father’s house —
NRSVCE.28: for I have five brothers — that he may warn them, so that they will not also come into this place of torment.’
NRSVCE.29: Abraham replied, ‘They have Moses and the prophets; they should listen to them.’
NRSVCE.30: He said, ‘No, father Abraham; but if someone goes to them from the dead, they will repent.’
NRSVCE.31: He said to him, ‘If they do not listen to Moses and the prophets, neither will they be convinced even if someone rises from the dead.’”

NET.1: Jesus also said to the disciples, “There was a rich man who was informed of accusations that his manager was wasting his assets.
NET.2: So he called the manager in and said to him, ‘What is this I hear about you? Turn in the account of your administration, because you can no longer be my manager.’
NET.3: Then the manager said to himself, ‘What should I do, since my master is taking my position away from me? I’m not strong enough to dig, and I’m too ashamed to beg.
NET.4: I know what to do so that when I am put out of management, people will welcome me into their homes.’
NET.5: So he contacted his master’s debtors one by one. He asked the first, ‘How much do you owe my master?’
NET.6: The man replied, ‘A hundred measures of olive oil.’ The manager said to him, ‘Take your bill, sit down quickly, and write fifty.’
NET.7: Then he said to another, ‘And how much do you owe?’ The second man replied, ‘A hundred measures of wheat.’ The manager said to him, ‘Take your bill, and write eighty.’
NET.8: The master commended the dishonest manager because he acted shrewdly. For the people of this world are more shrewd in dealing with their contemporaries than the people of light.
NET.9: And I tell you, make friends for yourselves by how you use worldly wealth, so that when it runs out you will be welcomed into the eternal homes.
NET.10: “The one who is faithful in a very little is also faithful in much, and the one who is dishonest in a very little is also dishonest in much.
NET.11: If then you haven’t been trustworthy in handling worldly wealth, who will entrust you with the true riches?
NET.12: And if you haven’t been trustworthy with someone else’s property, who will give you your own ?
NET.13: No servant can serve two masters, for either he will hate the one and love the other, or he will be devoted to the one and despise the other. You cannot serve God and money.”
NET.14: The Pharisees (who loved money) heard all this and ridiculed him.
NET.15: But Jesus said to them, “You are the ones who justify yourselves in men’s eyes, but God knows your hearts. For what is highly prized among men is utterly detestable in God’s sight.
NET.16: “The law and the prophets were in force until John; since then, the good news of the kingdom of God has been proclaimed, and everyone is urged to enter it.
NET.17: But it is easier for heaven and earth to pass away than for one tiny stroke of a letter in the law to become void.
NET.18: “Everyone who divorces his wife and marries someone else commits adultery, and the one who marries a woman divorced from her husband commits adultery.
NET.19: “There was a rich man who dressed in purple and fine linen and who feasted sumptuously every day.
NET.20: But at his gate lay a poor man named Lazarus whose body was covered with sores,
NET.21: who longed to eat what fell from the rich man’s table. In addition, the dogs came and licked his sores.
NET.22: “Now the poor man died and was carried by the angels to Abraham’s side. The rich man also died and was buried.
NET.23: And in hell, as he was in torment, he looked up and saw Abraham far off with Lazarus at his side.
NET.24: So he called out, ‘Father Abraham, have mercy on me, and send Lazarus to dip the tip of his finger in water and cool my tongue, because I am in anguish in this fire.’
NET.25: But Abraham said, ‘Child, remember that in your lifetime you received your good things and Lazarus likewise bad things, but now he is comforted here and you are in anguish.
NET.26: Besides all this, a great chasm has been fixed between us, so that those who want to cross over from here to you cannot do so, and no one can cross from there to us.’
NET.27: So the rich man said, ‘Then I beg you, father — send Lazarus to my father’s house
NET.28: (for I have five brothers) to warn them so that they don’t come into this place of torment.’
NET.29: But Abraham said, ‘They have Moses and the prophets; they must respond to them.’
NET.30: Then the rich man said, ‘No, father Abraham, but if someone from the dead goes to them, they will repent.’
NET.31: He replied to him, ‘If they do not respond to Moses and the prophets, they will not be convinced even if someone rises from the dead.’”
